title: Inspired
author: Marty Cagan
quote: It holds up really well. It was recommended to me by my first boss, and it's just got a bunch of great tips in there, just great foundational knowledge.
reason: The book every new PM should read — great foundational knowledge about how to create tech products people love.

title: Shape Up
author: Ryan Singer
reason: A phenomenal read — a practical framework for scoping and shipping product work without the endless backlog grind.

title: Hooked
author: Nir Eyal
quote: It was a nice sort of coming back to base a bit. The timing was perfect. It's a good solid read for now.
reason: A foundational read on behavioral design and how products build habits that keep users coming back.

title: Sprint
author: Jake Knapp
reason: A go-to for product and growth teams who want a structured, time-boxed way to test big ideas fast.

title: Getting Things Done
author: David Allen
reason: Jackie Bavaro's top productivity pick — a classic system for capturing and organizing everything on your plate.

title: Measure What Matters
author: John Doerr
reason: The canonical book on OKRs — essential reading for anyone trying to align teams around meaningful outcomes.

title: Creative Selection
author: Ken Kocienda
reason: A rare behind-the-scenes look at how Apple's early iPhone team iterated with taste — the best book on what it means to build great products.

title: Make Time
author: Jake Knapp
reason: Ben Williams says it radically changed his relationship with information — a practical guide to protecting what matters most in your day.

title: Outcomes Over Output
author: Joshua Seiden
reason: Petra Wille uses it constantly in coaching senior executives — a sharp framework for shifting teams from shipping features to driving real change.

title: Radical Candor
author: Kim Scott
quote: It's really good for management and best practices around giving feedback.
reason: Essential for managers who want a practical framework for giving honest, caring feedback — worth sending to everyone on your team.

title: Leaders Eat Last
author: Simon Sinek
reason: Melissa Tan's top leadership pick — a compelling case for why the best leaders put their teams first.

title: Leadership and Self-Deception
author: The Arbinger Institute
quote: It's a story about a person who has hit a wall and is getting feedback they don't know what to make of — it's around their mindset being stuck in a box. That was very powerful when I listened to it in my late 20s.
reason: A story-driven look at how we cause our own interpersonal problems by blaming others — and what we can actually do about it.

title: Scaling People
author: Claire Hughes Johnson
quote: It's just got an incredible amount of really useful tactical things, templates, all sorts of things that you can put into practice.
reason: Packed with tactical templates and frameworks that managers can put into practice immediately — worth sending to every new manager.

title: Five Dysfunctions of a Team
author: Patrick Lencioni
reason: Christina Wodtke recommends it to leaders who need to fix team dynamics at the top before their OKR process can work.

title: The 15 Commitments of Conscious Leadership
author: Jim Dethmer
reason: Jonny Miller calls it the best leadership book he has come across — practical and theoretically grounded, all about leading from openness rather than fear.

title: An Elegant Puzzle
author: Will Larson
reason: Alex Hardiman considers Stripe Press books like this one evergreen essentials — dense with hard-won lessons for engineering leaders.

title: High Growth Handbook
author: Elad Gil
reason: One of Alex Hardiman's go-to Stripe Press recommendations — a practical playbook for leaders navigating hypergrowth.

title: Bad Leadership
author: Barbara Kellerman
reason: Judd Antin loves that it studies the worst leaders rather than the best — understanding what makes leadership fail is just as valuable.

title: Good Strategy Bad Strategy
author: Richard Rumelt
quote: He's the best one I've ever seen for a definition of strategy.
reason: An essential starting point for anyone who wants to think more strategically — Rumelt's diagnosis-plus-action framework cuts through the noise.

title: The Innovator's Dilemma
author: Clayton Christensen
quote: Whether you're working at a large company suffering it or a startup trying to outflank an incumbent, the Innovator's Dilemma and its follow-on are the best books on product strategy to read.
reason: The best book on product strategy — whether you're suffering disruption at a large company or trying to outflank an incumbent as a startup.

title: Playing to Win
author: Roger Martin
reason: Richard Rumelt's top pick among strategy books — a rigorous framework for making real choices about where to play and how to win.

title: Crossing the Chasm
author: Geoffrey Moore
quote: It talks about how to get your first product on base — this concept of creating a beachhead. Marketing is something we don't talk about enough in product.
reason: Essential for any PM who needs to understand how to get a first product to market — the beachhead concept is foundational.

title: Only the Paranoid Survive
author: Andy Grove
reason: Richard Rumelt recommends reading it alongside biographies — Grove's firsthand account of navigating strategic inflection points is hard to beat.

title: The Goal
author: Eliyahu Goldratt
quote: It's essentially a novelization of the Toyota lean manufacturing system — a process engineering book written as a novel. Sales organizations are just revenue factories, so if you want to think about systems and processes without a textbook, it's absolutely fantastic.
reason: A novel that teaches the theory of constraints — the process engineering book disguised as a thriller that every operator should read.

title: The Phoenix Project
author: Gene Kim
reason: Will Larson describes it as a modern rewrite of The Goal applied to software engineering — essential for understanding how delivery systems actually work.

title: Thinking in Systems
author: Donella Meadows
quote: It's about really thinking through how systems work, and then you can start to extrapolate what is the second order effect of the system.
reason: Teaches how to intervene in systems at the right points rather than pushing on the wrong levers — a book multiple guests give to their teams.

title: Obviously Awesome
author: April Dunford
quote: I told April when she first wrote, I'm going to require every single founder I work with to read your book before we work with them — it's foundational, you have to know that.
reason: Required reading before launching anything — the go-to book on positioning that every marketer and PM should have read.

title: Purple Cow
author: Seth Godin
reason: Emily Kramer loves the classics that have stood the test of time — Godin's core lesson about building something truly remarkable is as relevant as ever.

title: The Tipping Point
author: Malcolm Gladwell
reason: One of Emily Kramer's classic marketing reads — a foundational look at how ideas and products spread and what triggers a tipping point.

title: Influence
author: Robert Cialdini
reason: Many people change careers after reading this — the definitive guide to the psychology of persuasion and why people say yes.

title: Predictably Irrational
author: Dan Ariely
reason: Kristen Berman calls it the best starter book for behavioral economics — the read that lit a lightbulb for her about how human psychology should drive product decisions.

title: Misbehaving
author: Richard Thaler
reason: Benjamin Lauzier loves the intersection of economics and human psychology — a great introduction to behavioral economics and how it applies to product.

title: Contagious
author: Jonah Berger
reason: Berger studied the best viral messages and boiled them down to a framework — recommended for anyone who wants to understand why things spread.

title: Never Split the Difference
author: Chris Voss
quote: It's about negotiations. There's actually also a masterclass on it.
reason: Useful far beyond negotiation — applies directly to business decisions, difficult conversations, and everyday life.

title: Decisive
author: Chip Heath
reason: Ethan Evans' number one recommendation — the science of making better decisions, applied equally to career moves and everyday life.

title: How to Measure Anything
author: Douglas Hubbard
reason: Reframes why we measure things at all — to reduce uncertainty for decisions, not to chase metrics everyone else is chasing.

title: Calling Bullshit
author: Carl Bergstrom
reason: Ronny Kohavi's top pick — embodies Twyman's law: if a result looks too extreme, your bullshit meter should go up.

title: Mistakes Were Made (But Not by Me)
author: Carol Tavris
reason: Ronny Kohavi loves this humbling look at the cognitive fallacies we all fall into — and why we are so good at blaming everyone but ourselves.

title: Switch
author: Chip Heath
reason: Yuhki Yamashita recommends it to anyone trying to drive organizational change — a practical guide to overcoming resistance and moving people.

title: Range
author: David Epstein
reason: Benjamin Lauzier recommends it to every generalist who has ever felt imposter syndrome — a compelling case for why breadth beats specialization.

title: Mindset
author: Carol Dweck
descriptor: Changed how I think about growth
quote: It kind of sounds like a self-help book, but then you understand that it's completely psychological and based on research — and that book completely changed my life.
reason: Zevi Arnovitz says it completely changed his life — sounds like self-help but it is grounded in research that exposes the fixed mindset holding you back.

title: Essentialism
author: Greg McKeown
reason: Yuriy Timen bought it for 40+ people on his team — a game-changer for learning to say no and find singular focus when everything feels urgent.

title: Four Thousand Weeks
author: Oliver Burkeman
reason: Georgiana Laudi was reading it when she came on the podcast — a book with nothing to do with marketing that she felt compelled to share anyway.

title: Man's Search for Meaning
author: Viktor Frankl
reason: Yuriy Timen calls it a remarkable memoir on perseverance — the core lesson is that you cannot control what happens around you, only your reaction to it.

title: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant
author: Eric Jorgenson
reason: Ethan Evans loves Naval's core idea: no one can be a better version of you, so figure out what you uniquely do best and love — that is your defensible career edge.

title: How Will You Measure Your Life
author: Clayton Christensen
reason: Meltem Kuran Berkowitz loves how Christensen takes his business frameworks and applies them to the decisions that actually matter in your life.

title: Quiet
author: Susan Cain
reason: A reminder that introverts have real power — and a call to adapt workplaces so the quietest voices are not left behind.

title: The Score Takes Care of Itself
author: Bill Walsh
reason: Pete Kazanjy's lesson from Bill Walsh: stop worrying about the scoreboard and focus on doing a high quantity of high-quality actions — the results follow.

title: Why We Sleep
author: Matthew Walker
reason: Logan Kilpatrick calls it one of the biggest up-levels you can do for yourself — the science of sleep is fascinating and the implications are hard to ignore.

title: Happy Money
author: Elizabeth Dunn
reason: Chris Hutchins loves this collection of research on the science of happier spending — a guide to optimizing how you use money for actual happiness.

title: Die with Zero
author: Bill Perkins
reason: Chris Hutchins says it had the biggest impact of any book he read recently — optimize for net fulfillment in life, not for accumulating money you never spend.

title: Project Hail Mary
author: Andy Weir
reason: Elena Verna says she read it in two days straight and could not put it down — a must for any sci-fi fan who loves problem-solving under pressure.

title: The Three-Body Problem
author: Liu Cixin
reason: One of Camille Hearst's top three books — a sweeping sci-fi saga that has captured the imagination of readers across the tech world.

title: Shoe Dog
author: Phil Knight
reason: Zevi Arnovitz calls it one of his favorite business books — an honest, gripping account of the grit and chaos behind building Nike from nothing.

title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
author: Gabrielle Zevin
reason: Hari Srinivasan passed it along after a friend recommended it — a phenomenal read about creativity, collaboration, and making games over a lifetime.

title: The Dream Machine
author: M. Mitchell Waldrop
reason: Boz calls it a tremendous missing piece of history — the story of how the modern computer came to be has profound resonance now that we are in the era of human-in-the-loop AI.

title: When Breath Becomes Air
author: Paul Kalanithi
reason: Geoff Charles recommends books that pull on your heartstrings and make you more human — this one does that more than almost any other.

title: All the Light We Cannot See
author: Anthony Doerr
reason: Emily Kramer's partner's favorite book, and now hers too — beautifully written and a reminder that great fiction can do what nonfiction cannot.

title: Demon Copperhead
author: Barbara Kingsolver
reason: Judd Antin calls it an outstanding read — a Pulitzer Prize winner that is sad, moving, and deeply illustrative of rural poverty in America.

title: Bird by Bird
author: Anne Lamott
reason: Eeke de Milliano calls it incredible and recommends it to PMs specifically — because great product managers need to be great writers.

title: Where Good Ideas Come From
author: Steven Johnson
reason: Sam Schillace loves the adjacent possible concept — a book about how innovation really happens that he finds just as timely now as when it came out.

title: High Output Management
author: Andy Grove
reason: Classic management book on getting the most out of teams; consistently the most-recommended management book across all episodes.

title: The Design of Everyday Things
author: Don Norman
reason: Foundational book on product and physical design principles; multiple guests say it changed how they see the world.

title: The Hard Thing About Hard Things
author: Ben Horowitz
reason: How to manage your psychology when things get hard as a founder or executive.

title: Zero to One
author: Peter Thiel
reason: Classic startup book on building monopolies and truly unique companies.

title: The Lean Startup
author: Eric Ries
reason: Foundational startup methodology; particularly valuable for its cohort analysis lessons.

title: Good to Great
author: Jim Collins
reason: Most influential management book for understanding what makes companies exceptional; helped Amazon codify its flywheel thinking.

title: 7 Powers
author: Hamilton Helmer
reason: Best book on business strategy; framework for building durable competitive advantage.

title: Competing Against Luck
author: Clayton Christensen
reason: Jobs-to-be-done framework; helps you understand why people hire your product.

title: Thinking in Bets
author: Annie Duke
reason: Framework for better decision-making under uncertainty; teaches you to separate outcome quality from decision quality.

title: What You Do Is Who You Are
author: Ben Horowitz
reason: Building interesting, engaging company cultures through actions and behaviors rather than words.

title: Never Search Alone
author: Phyl Terry
reason: The most impactful book referenced for job searching; introduces the job search councils methodology.

title: Great by Choice
author: Jim Collins
reason: Really good frameworks for building businesses under uncertainty.

title: Founding Sales
author: Pete Kazanjy
reason: Lenny's go-to recommendation for every founder trying to figure out sales for the first time.

title: Dynamic Reteaming
author: Heidi Helfand
reason: The art and wisdom of changing teams; reframes reorgs as opportunities rather than disruptions.

title: The Making of a Manager
author: Julie Zhuo
reason: Bestselling guide for first-time managers; one of Lenny's all-time favorite management reads.

title: Difficult Conversations
author: Douglas Stone, Bruce Patton, Sheila Heen
reason: How to discuss what matters most; from Lenny's all-time favorite reads newsletter.

title: The Effective Executive
author: Peter Drucker
reason: Management fundamentals; Bill Carr's top recommendation alongside Good to Great.

title: Atomic Habits
author: James Clear
reason: Framework for building and changing habits; winners and losers have the same goals — systems are what differ.

title: Deep Work
author: Cal Newport
reason: One of the most important professional development books Lenny has read; focused uninterrupted work is the source of real value.

title: Indistractable
author: Nir Eyal
reason: Identity-based approach to becoming the kind of person who follows through on intentions.

title: Daily Stoic
author: Ryan Holiday
reason: Timothy Davis reads it every day; a year of daily stoic philosophy excerpts and reflections.

title: Storyworthy
author: Matthew Dicks
reason: Lenny's all-time favorite book on storytelling; he couldn't put it down and says every marketer should read it.

title: On Writing Well
author: William Zinsser
reason: Classic guide to writing nonfiction; from Lenny's all-time favorite reads newsletter.

title: On Writing: A Memoir of the Craft
author: Stephen King
reason: Memoir and writing guide from Stephen King; from Lenny's all-time favorite reads newsletter.

title: The Fountainhead
author: Ayn Rand
reason: One of his favorite books; really makes you think and feel about individualism and creative integrity.

title: The Blind Assassin
author: Margaret Atwood
reason: Fiction book she has been enjoying and recommending recently.

title: The Color Purple
author: Alice Walker
reason: One of Kim Scott's very favorite books; she recommends reading more fiction and novels alongside business books.

title: Ender's Game
author: Orson Scott Card
reason: Classic sci-fi; Lenny has read the entire series and both he and Sander love it.

title: The Selfish Gene
author: Richard Dawkins
reason: Changed the way they see the world; also one of Lenny's personal favorites.

title: The Road Less Traveled
author: M. Scott Peck
reason: Classic human psychology book; Jason Droege has read it more than once.

title: Aurora
author: David Koepp
reason: Fiction about an electromagnetic pulse that knocks out the power grid; a favorite pick from Robby Stein.

title: The Mom Test
author: Rob Fitzpatrick
reason: Great book on how to talk to customers and validate ideas; from Lenny's all-time favorite reads newsletter.

title: Don't Make Me Think
author: Steve Krug
reason: Classic UX principle; Stewart Butterfield still uses it as a guiding mantra.

title: Cracking the PM Interview
author: Jackie Bavaro, Gayle Laakmann McDowell
reason: PM interview preparation; from Lenny's all-time favorite reads newsletter.

title: Start at the End
author: Matt Waller
reason: Behavioral science approach to thinking clearly about what you're actually building and for whom.

title: Four Steps to the Epiphany
author: Steve Blank
reason: The pre-Lean Startup manual for market validation; was guiding early startup teams before Eric Ries popularized the methodology.

title: The Fund
author: Rob Copeland
reason: Kim Scott told Lenny he must read it; all about Bridgewater's deeply problematic culture.

title: Failing Forward
author: John Maxwell
reason: One of Inbal Shani's top book recommendations.

title: Dare to Lead Like a Girl
author: Dalia Feldheim
reason: A recent read Inbal Shani recommended on the show.

title: The Black Swan
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
reason: One of Brian Tolkin's top book recommendations alongside Shoe Dog and The Design of Everyday Things.

title: The Fire Next Time
author: James Baldwin
reason: Nikita Miller recommends almost anything by James Baldwin; most recently reread this one.

title: Steve Jobs
author: Walter Isaacson
reason: Richard Rumelt called it brilliant; recommends biographies and histories including this one.

title: The Upside of Stress
author: Kelly McGonigal
reason: Keith named this his number one book recommendation, saying it makes a compelling, evidence-backed argument that more stress in your life leads to greater happiness, health, and wealth. He called it transformative.

title: The Jordan Rules
author: Sam Smith
reason: Keith recommended it as a counterintuitive read on high-performance culture, arguing that high-performance machines are about winning, not psychological safety, and that aspiring leaders should model how Michael Jordan actually operated.

title: Creativity, Inc.
author: Ed Catmull
reason: Lenny referenced the book's concept of the ugly baby — the idea that every great Pixar idea starts as something rough and unappealing that people want to dismiss — to illustrate how contrarian early-stage bets can be the best ones.
